pathways.label: No-conviction expungement for acquittal, dismissal, diversion, or deferred adjudication
pathways.summary: A person charged under West Virginia law who was found not guilty or whose charges were dismissed, and not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another offense resulting in conviction, may file a civil petition in the circuit court where the charges were filed. The petition seeks expungement of records relating to the arrest, charge, or related matters. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/") A person whose charges were dismissed after successful completion of pretrial diversion under 61-11-22 or deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a may also petition for expungement of all charges originally brought if the charges arose from the…
ruleClauses: A person charged under West Virginia law who was found not guilty or whose charges were dismissed, and not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another offense resulting in conviction, may file a civil petition in the circuit court where the charges were filed. The petition seeks expungement of records relating to the arrest, charge, or related matters. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")

A person whose charges were dismissed after successful completion of pretrial diversion under 61-11-22 or deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a may also petition for expungement of all charges originally brought if the charges arose from the same transaction or occurrence. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
Timing: the petition may be filed no sooner than 60 days after the courts order of acquittal or dismissal. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
Fee rule: no filing fees or costs are charged for a 61-11-25 action. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")

Important exclusions: A person with a prior felony conviction may not file under 61-11-25, and criminal investigation reports plus certain records related to offenses subject to sex-offender-registration provisions are exempt from this section. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")

West Virginia pretrial diversion agreements are written agreements with the prosecuting attorney and generally may not exceed 24 months. If the person successfully complies, they are not subject to prosecution for the covered offense unless the agreement includes a plea to a specific related offense. DUI under 17C-5-2 is excluded from pretrial diversion, and several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are also excluded. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22/")

Deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a can last up to three years for a felony and up to two years for a misdemeanor. If the person complies with court-imposed terms, they may withdraw the guilty plea and the case is dismissed, or, if agreed by the parties and court, enter a plea to a lesser offense. Several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are excluded or heavily restricted. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22A/")

If your case was dismissed after diversion or deferred adjudication, that is not the same as a conviction expungement. West Virginia has a no-conviction expungement route, but we need the dismissal order and must confirm the case was not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another conviction.

No-conviction expungement: acquittal, dismissal, diversion, deferred adjudication A person charged under West Virginia law who was found not guilty or whose charges were dismissed, and not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another offense resulting in conviction, may file a civil petition in the circuit court where the charges were filed.
West Virginia has a no-conviction expungement route, but we need the dismissal order and must confirm the case was not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another conviction.
waitingRules: Timing: the petition may be filed no sooner than 60 days after the courts order of acquittal or dismissal. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")

If the person successfully complies, they are not subject to prosecution for the covered offense unless the agreement includes a plea to a specific related offense. DUI under 17C-5-2 is excluded from pretrial diversion, and several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are also excluded. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22/")

Deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a can last up to three years for a felony and up to two years for a misdemeanor. If the person complies with court-imposed terms, they may withdraw the guilty plea and the case is dismissed, or, if agreed by the parties and court, enter a plea to a lesser offense. Several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are excluded or heavily restricted. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22A/")

A person is not eligible under 61-11-26 for convictions including: Felony offense of violence against the person. Misdemeanor involving intentional physical injury to a minor or law-enforcement officer. Felony offense where the victim was a minor. Sex offenses under 61-8B-1 et seq. Any offense where the petitioner used or exhibited a deadly weapon or dangerous instrument. Domestic battery/domestic assault-related offenses and certain assault/battery offenses involving a spouse, co-parent, or cohabitant. Strangulation. DUI. Driving while license revoked for DUI. Certain child, abuse, sexual, dwelling burglary, sexually motivated, CDL, attempt, or conspiracy offenses listed in the statute. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/") A DUI conviction does not automatically block expungement of an unrelated otherwise expungable felony if the DUI is at least five years old when the expungement petition is filed. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

The petitioner must serve the petition and supporting documentation on the Superintendent of the State Police, the prosecutor, the arresting law-enforcement agency, any institution where the petitioner was confined, and the court that disposed of the criminal charge. The prosecutor serves the petition and proposed order on any identified victims. Opposing parties have 30 days to file opposition, and the petitioner may reply within 30 days after service of opposition. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

A person is not eligible until one year after being pardoned and five years after discharge of the sentence for the pardoned conviction. Pardon-based expungement is not available for first-degree murder, treason, kidnapping, or felony sex offenses under Article 8B. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/5-1-16A/")

West Virginia juvenile proceeding records are generally not public records. One year after the juveniles 18th birthday, or one year after personal or juvenile jurisdiction ends, whichever is later, juvenile records may be kept in a separate secure confidential place and may not be inspected except by circuit court order. Marking juvenile records confidential has the legal effect of extinguishing the offense as if it never occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/49-5-104/")

pathways.label: Eligible conviction expungement under W. Va. Code § 61-11-26
pathways.summary: West Virginia allows a person convicted of a misdemeanor offense or offenses to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. It also allows a person convicted of a nonviolent felony offense or offenses arising from the same transaction or series of transactions to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/") A 61-11-26 petition must be verified under oath and include the petitioners names and aliases, addresses since the offense, date of birth, Social Security number, arrest date, court, case number, statutes and offenses charged/convicted, victim information,…
pathways.sourceRef: section:59-60; section:147-152; section:11-24; section:6-10; section:157-162; section:163-167; corpus-unit:10; overview:6-10
ruleClauses: West Virginia allows a person convicted of a misdemeanor offense or offenses to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. It also allows a person convicted of a nonviolent felony offense or offenses arising from the same transaction or series of transactions to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

A 61-11-26 petition must be verified under oath and include the petitioners names and aliases, addresses since the offense, date of birth, Social Security number, arrest date, court, case number, statutes and offenses charged/convicted, victim information, restitution/protection/no-contact order information, disposition and sentence, grounds for expungement, rehabilitation steps, prior expungement history, and supporting documents. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

The petitioner must serve the petition and supporting documentation on the Superintendent of the State Police, the prosecutor, the arresting law-enforcement agency, any institution where the petitioner was confined, and the court that disposed of the criminal charge. The prosecutor serves the petition and proposed order on any identified victims. Opposing parties have 30 days to file opposition, and the petitioner may reply within 30 days after service of opposition. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

Do not generate a West Virginia conviction packet without the arresting agency, court of disposition, confinement institution if any, prosecutor address, victim/no-contact/restoration information, and rehabilitation statement.

West Virginia uses the word expungement, but the practical effect is removal from public access and sealing/expungement of records held by courts and agencies. For conviction expungement, the statute defines expungement as removal from public records of evidence that a person was charged or convicted, except records specifically exempted by statute. It also defines sealing as removing court, website, and law-enforcement records from public inspection or public access. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

In West Virginia, expungement can remove a qualifying record from public access, but it is not a magic erase button. Some sealed records can still be accessed by court order or by legally authorized criminal-history checks.

If the court grants 61-11-26 relief, it orders court records sealed and agency/law-enforcement records expunged. Agencies ordered to expunge must certify completion to the court within 60 days after entry of the order. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

After expungement, the matter is generally considered never to have occurred, courts and agencies reply that no record exists, and the person generally does not have to disclose the record on employment, credit, or other applications. However, applicants for jobs involving prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution, or incarceration of law violators must disclose expunged convictions, and entities required by state or federal law to obtain criminal-history checks may know about expunged convictions. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

Expungement can let you answer most ordinary applications as if the record does not exist, but there are exceptions. Law-enforcement/criminal-justice jobs and legally required background checks may still require or reveal the conviction.

For 61-11-26 conviction expungement, the circuit clerk charges the same fee as filing a civil action, and a person who receives an expungement order must pay a $100 West Virginia State Police processing fee. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
For 61-11-25 acquittal/dismissal/diversion/deferred-adjudication expungement, no filing fees or costs are charged. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
For 61-11-26a accelerated treatment/job-readiness expungement, the $100 State Police processing fee is waived. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26A/")

6. Conviction expungement: 61-11-26 A. What convictions can be considered West Virginia allows a person convicted of a misdemeanor offense or offenses to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. It also allows a person convicted of a nonviolent felony offense or offenses arising from the same transaction or series of transactions to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/") B. Waiting periods

Petition contents and service for conviction expungement A 61-11-26 petition must be verified under oath and include the petitioners names and aliases, addresses since the offense, date of birth, Social Security number, arrest date, court, case number, statutes and offenses charged/convicted, victim information, restitution/protection/no-contact order information, disposition and sentence, grounds for expungement, rehabilitation steps, prior expungement history, and supporting documents. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/") The petitioner must serve the petition and supporting documentation on the Superintendent of the State Police, the prosecutor, the arresting law-enforcement agency, any institution where the petitioner was confined, and the court that disposed of the criminal charge.

Burden of proof and court procedure For 61-11-26 conviction expungement, the petitioner bears the burden by clear and convincing evidence to prove the conviction is the only conviction for the specified offense/offenses, is not excluded, the required time has passed, no criminal charges are pending, expungement is consistent with public welfare, the petitioner has been rehabilitated and law-abiding, and any other facts the court considers necessary. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/") Within 60 days after filing, the circuit court must either grant the petition, return it for missing information or obvious errors, set it for hearing, or deny it if the petition shows the person is not legally entitled.

Category Accelerated timing under 61-11-26a
Single misdemeanor After sentence/supervision completion and 90 days of approved treatment/recovery compliance or job-readiness completion
Multiple misdemeanors 1 year after last conviction, incarceration completion, or supervision completion, whichever is later
Eligible nonviolent felony 3 years after conviction, incarceration completion, or supervision completion, whichever is later

Petitions under this route must include treatment/recovery documentation and/or a certificate of graduation from the approved job-readiness course, and the $100 West Virginia State Police processing fee is waived for 61-11-26a petitions. (

9. Accelerated expungement: treatment/recovery or job-readiness route West Virginia has an accelerated route for a person otherwise eligible under 61-11-26 who has either a medically documented history of substance abuse and successful compliance with an approved treatment/recovery/counseling program, or completion of a West Virginia Department of Education-approved job-readiness adult training course. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26A/")

West Virginia pretrial diversion agreements are written agreements with the prosecuting attorney and generally may not exceed 24 months. If the person successfully complies, they are not subject to prosecution for the covered offense unless the agreement includes a plea to a specific related offense. DUI under 17C-5-2 is excluded from pretrial diversion, and several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are also excluded. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22/")

Deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a can last up to three years for a felony and up to two years for a misdemeanor. If the person complies with court-imposed terms, they may withdraw the guilty plea and the case is dismissed, or, if agreed by the parties and court, enter a plea to a lesser offense. Several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are excluded or heavily restricted. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22A/")

A person is not eligible under 61-11-26 for convictions including: Felony offense of violence against the person. Misdemeanor involving intentional physical injury to a minor or law-enforcement officer. Felony offense where the victim was a minor. Sex offenses under 61-8B-1 et seq. Any offense where the petitioner used or exhibited a deadly weapon or dangerous instrument. Domestic battery/domestic assault-related offenses and certain assault/battery offenses involving a spouse, co-parent, or cohabitant. Strangulation. DUI. Driving while license revoked for DUI. Certain child, abuse, sexual, dwelling burglary, sexually motivated, CDL, attempt, or conspiracy offenses listed in the statute. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/") A DUI conviction does not automatically block expungement of an unrelated otherwise expungable felony if the DUI is at least five years old when the expungement petition is filed. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

The petitioner must serve the petition and supporting documentation on the Superintendent of the State Police, the prosecutor, the arresting law-enforcement agency, any institution where the petitioner was confined, and the court that disposed of the criminal charge. The prosecutor serves the petition and proposed order on any identified victims. Opposing parties have 30 days to file opposition, and the petitioner may reply within 30 days after service of opposition. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

A person is not eligible until one year after being pardoned and five years after discharge of the sentence for the pardoned conviction. Pardon-based expungement is not available for first-degree murder, treason, kidnapping, or felony sex offenses under Article 8B. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/5-1-16A/")

West Virginia juvenile proceeding records are generally not public records. One year after the juveniles 18th birthday, or one year after personal or juvenile jurisdiction ends, whichever is later, juvenile records may be kept in a separate secure confidential place and may not be inspected except by circuit court order. Marking juvenile records confidential has the legal effect of extinguishing the offense as if it never occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/49-5-104/")
Single misdemeanor 1 year after conviction, completion of incarceration, or completion of supervision, whichever is later
Multiple misdemeanors 2 years after last conviction, completion of incarceration, or completion of supervision for the last conviction, whichever is later
Nonviolent felony 5 years after conviction, completion of incarceration, or completion of supervision, whichever is later

pathways.label: Pardon-based expungement
pathways.summary: A person who receives a full and unconditional pardon from the Governor may petition the circuit court in the county of conviction to expunge the conviction. The petitioner must serve the prosecutor and publish a Class I legal advertisement notice. The circuit court may enter expungement after verifying the pardon and finding good cause. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/5-1-16A/") A person is not eligible until one year after being pardoned and five years after discharge of the sentence for the pardoned conviction. Pardon-based expungement is not available for first-degree murder, treason, kidnapping, or felony sex offenses under Article 8B.…

pathways.label: Sex-trafficking-victim vacatur and expungement
pathways.summary: A person convicted of prostitution, or adjudicated delinquent, as a direct result of being a trafficking victim may petition the circuit court in the county of conviction or juvenile adjudication to vacate the conviction/adjudication and expunge the record. The court may grant relief if it finds the persons participation in the offense was a direct result of being trafficked. No rehabilitation requirement is imposed for this trafficking-victim expungement route. 2 Proof at least one year since pardon.

West Virginia uses the word expungement, but the practical effect is removal from public access and sealing/expungement of records held by courts and agencies. For conviction expungement, the statute defines expungement as removal from public records of evidence that a person was charged or convicted, except records specifically exempted by statute. It also defines sealing as removing court, website, and law-enforcement records from public inspection or public access. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

In West Virginia, expungement can remove a qualifying record from public access, but it is not a magic erase button. Some sealed records can still be accessed by court order or by legally authorized criminal-history checks.

Misdemeanor involving intentional physical injury to a minor or law-enforcement officer. Felony offense where the victim was a minor. Sex offenses under 61-8B-1 et seq. Pardon-based expungement is not available for first-degree murder, treason, kidnapping, or felony sex offenses under Article 8B. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/5-1-16A/")

sourceSections.title: Core West Virginia rule
sourceSections.text:
West Virginia uses the word expungement, but the practical effect is removal from public access and sealing/expungement of records held by courts and agencies. For conviction expungement, the statute defines expungement as removal from public records of evidence that a person was charged or convicted, except records specifically exempted by statute. It also defines sealing as removing court, website, and law-enforcement records from public inspection or public access. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
Wilma plain-language script:
In West Virginia, expungement can remove a qualifying record from public access, but it is not a magic erase button. Some sealed records can still be accessed by court order or by legally authorized criminal-history checks.

sourceSections.title: No-conviction expungement: acquittal, dismissal, diversion, deferred adjudication
sourceSections.text:
A person charged under West Virginia law who was found not guilty or whose charges were dismissed, and not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another offense resulting in conviction, may file a civil petition in the circuit court where the charges were filed. The petition seeks expungement of records relating to the arrest, charge, or related matters. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
A person whose charges were dismissed after successful completion of pretrial diversion under 61-11-22 or deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a may also petition for expungement of all charges originally brought if the charges arose from the same transaction or occurrence. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
Timing: the petition may be filed no sooner than 60 days after the courts order of acquittal or dismissal. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
Fee rule: no filing fees or costs are charged for a 61-11-25 action. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
Wilma status labels:

likely_eligible_dismissal_60_days

Important exclusions: A person with a prior felony conviction may not file under 61-11-25, and criminal investigation reports plus certain records related to offenses subject to sex-offender-registration provisions are exempt from this section. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")

sourceSections.title: Pretrial diversion and deferred adjudication context
sourceSections.text:
West Virginia pretrial diversion agreements are written agreements with the prosecuting attorney and generally may not exceed 24 months. If the person successfully complies, they are not subject to prosecution for the covered offense unless the agreement includes a plea to a specific related offense. DUI under 17C-5-2 is excluded from pretrial diversion, and several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are also excluded. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22/")
Deferred adjudication under 61-11-22a can last up to three years for a felony and up to two years for a misdemeanor. If the person complies with court-imposed terms, they may withdraw the guilty plea and the case is dismissed, or, if agreed by the parties and court, enter a plea to a lesser offense. Several violence, sex, child, strangulation, domestic battery, and household-member offenses are excluded or heavily restricted. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-22A/")
Wilma script:
If your case was dismissed after diversion or deferred adjudication, that is not the same as a conviction expungement. West Virginia has a no-conviction expungement route, but we need the dismissal order and must confirm the case was not dismissed in exchange for a guilty plea to another conviction.

sourceSections.title: What convictions can be considered
sourceSections.text: West Virginia allows a person convicted of a misdemeanor offense or offenses to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. It also allows a person convicted of a nonviolent felony offense or offenses arising from the same transaction or series of transactions to petition in the circuit court where the conviction occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Conviction category Waiting period
Single misdemeanor 1 year after conviction, completion of incarceration, or completion of supervision, whichever is later
Multiple misdemeanors 2 years after last conviction, completion of incarceration, or completion of supervision for the last conviction, whichever is later
Nonviolent felony 5 years after conviction, completion of incarceration, or completion of supervision, whichever is later
These waiting periods are directly set by 61-11-26(b). (

"https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")

sourceSections.title: Accelerated expungement: treatment/recovery or job-readiness route
sourceSections.text:
West Virginia has an accelerated route for a person otherwise eligible under 61-11-26 who has either a medically documented history of substance abuse and successful compliance with an approved treatment/recovery/counseling program, or completion of a West Virginia Department of Education-approved job-readiness adult training course. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26A/")

Category Accelerated timing under 61-11-26a
Single misdemeanor After sentence/supervision completion and 90 days of approved treatment/recovery compliance or job-readiness completion
Multiple misdemeanors 1 year after last conviction, incarceration completion, or supervision completion, whichever is later
Eligible nonviolent felony 3 years after conviction, incarceration completion, or supervision completion, whichever is later
Petitions under this route must include treatment/recovery documentation and/or a certificate of graduation from the approved job-readiness course, and the $100 West Virginia State Police processing fee is waived for 61-11-26a petitions. (

"https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26A/")

sourceSections.title: Petition contents and service for conviction expungement
sourceSections.text:
A 61-11-26 petition must be verified under oath and include the petitioners names and aliases, addresses since the offense, date of birth, Social Security number, arrest date, court, case number, statutes and offenses charged/convicted, victim information, restitution/protection/no-contact order information, disposition and sentence, grounds for expungement, rehabilitation steps, prior expungement history, and supporting documents. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
The petitioner must serve the petition and supporting documentation on the Superintendent of the State Police, the prosecutor, the arresting law-enforcement agency, any institution where the petitioner was confined, and the court that disposed of the criminal charge. The prosecutor serves the petition and proposed order on any identified victims. Opposing parties have 30 days to file opposition, and the petitioner may reply within 30 days after service of opposition. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
Wilma packet warning:
Do not generate a West Virginia conviction packet without the arresting agency, court of disposition, confinement institution if any, prosecutor address, victim/no-contact/restoration information, and rehabilitation statement.

sourceSections.title: Effect of conviction expungement
sourceSections.text:
If the court grants 61-11-26 relief, it orders court records sealed and agency/law-enforcement records expunged. Agencies ordered to expunge must certify completion to the court within 60 days after entry of the order. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
After expungement, the matter is generally considered never to have occurred, courts and agencies reply that no record exists, and the person generally does not have to disclose the record on employment, credit, or other applications. However, applicants for jobs involving prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution, or incarceration of law violators must disclose expunged convictions, and entities required by state or federal law to obtain criminal-history checks may know about expunged convictions. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
Wilma plain-language script:
Expungement can let you answer most ordinary applications as if the record does not exist, but there are exceptions. Law-enforcement/criminal-justice jobs and legally required background checks may still require or reveal the conviction.

sourceSections.title: Fees
sourceSections.text:
For 61-11-26 conviction expungement, the circuit clerk charges the same fee as filing a civil action, and a person who receives an expungement order must pay a $100 West Virginia State Police processing fee. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26/")
For 61-11-25 acquittal/dismissal/diversion/deferred-adjudication expungement, no filing fees or costs are charged. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-25/")
For 61-11-26a accelerated treatment/job-readiness expungement, the $100 State Police processing fee is waived. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-11-26A/")

sourceSections.title: Pardon-based expungement
sourceSections.text:
A person who receives a full and unconditional pardon from the Governor may petition the circuit court in the county of conviction to expunge the conviction. The petitioner must serve the prosecutor and publish a Class I legal advertisement notice. The circuit court may enter expungement after verifying the pardon and finding good cause. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/5-1-16A/")
A person is not eligible until one year after being pardoned and five years after discharge of the sentence for the pardoned conviction. Pardon-based expungement is not available for first-degree murder, treason, kidnapping, or felony sex offenses under Article 8B. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/5-1-16A/")

sourceSections.title: Sex-trafficking victim vacatur and expungement
sourceSections.text:
A person convicted of prostitution, or adjudicated delinquent, as a direct result of being a trafficking victim may petition the circuit court in the county of conviction or juvenile adjudication to vacate the conviction/adjudication and expunge the record. The court may grant relief if it finds the persons participation in the offense was a direct result of being trafficked. No rehabilitation requirement is imposed for this trafficking-victim expungement route. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/61-14-9/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_trafficking_victim_vacatur_expungement
legal_aid_handoff_trafficking_survivor

Wilma script:
West Virginia has a special relief route for prostitution records caused by sex trafficking. This can involve vacating the conviction or juvenile adjudication, so it should be routed to legal aid or an attorney.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile records
sourceSections.text:
West Virginia juvenile proceeding records are generally not public records. One year after the juveniles 18th birthday, or one year after personal or juvenile jurisdiction ends, whichever is later, juvenile records may be kept in a separate secure confidential place and may not be inspected except by circuit court order. Marking juvenile records confidential has the legal effect of extinguishing the offense as if it never occurred. ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/49-5-104/")
Important limits: Juvenile records for a juvenile convicted under adult criminal jurisdiction may not be marked and kept confidential under 49-5-104(e). ("https://code.wvlegislature.gov/49-5-104/")

Generate/check:
1 SCA-C903 - Motion for Expungement of Criminal Records Due to Acquittal or Dismissal for Reasons Other than Entry of a Plea.
2 Certified dismissal or acquittal order.
3 Diversion or deferred adjudication completion/dismissal order, if applicable.
4 Statement confirming dismissal was not in exchange for guilty plea to another conviction.
5 Statement confirming no current charges/proceedings are pending for the matter.
6 Proposed order.
7 Service/notice to prosecuting attorney and arresting agency if hearing is set.
Misdemeanor conviction packet
Generate/check:
1 SCA-C906 - Petition for Expungement of Misdemeanor Violations and Traffic Citations.
2 Verified petition under oath.
3 Full identity/address history.
4 Arrest, court, case, statute, conviction, disposition, and sentence details.
5 Victim/no-victim statement.
6 Restitution/protection/no-contact order disclosure and attachments.
7 Rehabilitation statement.
8 Prior expungement disclosure.
9 Service to State Police, prosecutor, arresting agency, confinement institution if any, and disposing court.
10 Proposed order.
Felony conviction packet
Generate/check:
1 SCA-C907 - Petition for Expungement of Felony Violations.
2 Nonviolent-felony analysis.
3 Same-transaction/series analysis if multiple felony offenses.
4 Exclusion checklist.
5 Waiting-period proof.
6 Pending-charge check.
7 Rehabilitation proof.
8 Service and proposed order.
Accelerated treatment/job-readiness packet
Add to the misdemeanor or felony packet:
1 Medical documentation of substance-abuse history, if relying on treatment/recovery route.
2 Proof of successful compliance with approved treatment/recovery/counseling program.
3 West Virginia Department of Education-approved job-readiness adult training certificate, if relying on that route.
4 State Police fee-waiver notation under 61-11-26a.
First-offense drug possession conditional discharge packet
Generate/check:
1 Conditional discharge order under 60A-4-407.
2 Proof probation completed.
3 Date probation expired.
4 Six-month waiting-period calculation.
5 Proof no serious or repeated probation violations.
6 Proposed order expunging official records of arrest, trial, and conviction under 60A-4-407.
Pardon packet
Generate/check:
1 Full and unconditional Governor pardon.
2 Proof at least one year since pardon.
3 Proof at least five years since sentence discharge.
4 Publication notice/Class I legal advertisement.
5 Service to prosecutor.
6 Good-cause statement.
7 Exclusion check: first-degree murder, treason, kidnapping, felony Article 8B sex offense.

sourceSections.title: Attorney/legal-aid referral triggers
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend legal review if:
Any felony conviction.
Multiple felonies or unclear same-transaction/series issue.
Any violence, domestic violence, household-member, strangulation, sex, child-victim, deadly-weapon, dwelling-burglary, or DUI-related issue.
Any CDL or commercial-motor-vehicle issue.
Any pending charges.
Any protection, no-contact, restitution, or restraining order.
Any identified victim may oppose.
The user previously received expungement relief.
The user needs pardon-based relief.
The user is a trafficking survivor seeking vacatur/expungement.
The user asks about firearm rights, immigration, professional licensing, law enforcement/corrections employment, childcare, healthcare, security clearance, or federal records.
The case involves federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state records.

21. Wilma
never say
rules
Wilma must not say:

You are definitely eligible.

The court must grant this,
except where the statute clearly makes relief mandatory and facts are confirmed.

This destroys every record.

No one can ever see it again.

You never have to disclose it anywhere.

This restores firearm rights.

This fixes immigration consequences.

A DUI can be expunged like any other misdemeanor.

A CDL traffic case can be masked.

Dismissal always qualifies,
without checking whether it was in exchange for a guilty plea to another conviction.

You can file as many conviction expungements as you want.
